source_label: Bitcoin Optech — Topics on JIT Channels
excerpt: A JIT channel is opened in response to an incoming HTLC rather than in response to an explicit channel-purchase request. The LSP intercepts the HTLC, opens a channel to the recipient using its own liquidity, and forwards the HTLC over the new channel. The recipient receives the payment and gets a working channel as a side effect; the LSP recovers its capital from the forwarded amount and a fee.
body:
Source excerpt:
A JIT channel is opened in response to an incoming HTLC rather than in response to an explicit channel-purchase request. The LSP intercepts the HTLC, opens a channel to the recipient using its own liquidity, and forwards the HTLC over the new channel. The recipient receives the payment and gets a working channel as a side effect; the LSP recovers its capital from the forwarded amount and a fee.

---

Cited by leaf "LSPs, JIT channels, liquidity ads":
The bootstrapping problem for a new Lightning user is concrete: to *receive* a payment, you need an existing channel with inbound liquidity (someone else's capital pointing toward you), and you can't have one without convincing a counterparty to open one for you. A *Lightning Service Provider* (LSP) is a routing node that solves this by opening a channel to a new user on demand, often as part of the user's first-receive experience, in exchange for a small fee. The user gets working Lightning service in seconds; the LSP gets a paying customer and a new edge in its routing graph [1].

The mechanism that makes this safe without custody is the *JIT channel*. The LSP intercepts an incoming HTLC bound for a peer the user doesn't yet have a channel with, opens a channel to the user *before* settling the HTLC, and uses the incoming funds to back the new channel's commitment. The user's wallet sees a normal channel-funded balance; the LSP recovers its outlay from the routed HTLC. If anything fails — the LSP can't open the channel, the user is offline at the wrong moment, the chain fee for the funding transaction spikes too high — the HTLC times out and the original sender's funds are returned. Custody is never transferred [2].

The LSP specs are coordinated under the *LSPS* (Lightning Service Provider Specifications) umbrella as part of the BLIP-51 family. LSPS1 standardises the channel-purchase API: a wallet asks an LSP for a channel of size X with a specified push amount, the LSP quotes a fee, the wallet pays via Lightning, the channel opens. LSPS2 standardises JIT-channel HTLC interception. Wallets that implement the spec — Phoenix, Breez, Zeus, Mutiny — can talk to any compliant LSP without per-LSP integration work, which is the precondition for a competitive LSP market.

*Liquidity ads* are the broader market layer. A node that wants inbound liquidity can post an offer ("I'll pay X sats for an inbound channel of Y sats from any node willing to open one"), and any willing node can accept. BOLT-12 offers and the proposed liquidity-ads extension formalise this so that liquidity becomes a fungible thing nodes can buy and sell, not a relationship between specific operators. This is what turns Lightning from a federation of well-connected hubs into something closer to a market for routing capacity [3].

The LSP layer is also where Lightning's UX problems get solved. An LSP that integrates with a wallet handles channel-management, fee-bumping, splicing, watchtower service, and on-chain swaps so that the end user only ever sees "send" and "receive". This abstraction is what makes Lightning usable at scale — at the cost of introducing a new dependency on a usually-identifiable service provider. Operators who care about adversarial scenarios run their own node and skip the LSP layer; everyone else trades a small amount of custody-adjacent risk and a small recurring fee for a working wallet on a smartphone.
